narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and LSAT score of 29, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 5 and 3, respectively, and factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and undergraduate GPA is 3.24, with Year 1 deciles averaging 6.04 and Year 3 at 5.91, giving you a sense of where your profile stands relative to successful peers. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were, first, your Year 3 decile ranking, which was notably lower than typical for bar passers and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a favorable pull toward predicting success since it aligned better with passing students' averages. Your Year 1 decile also nudged the prediction positively, reflecting a solid early foundation compared to those who passed. While your full-time status matches the norm for successful students (93.2% of passers), and your family income falls in the middle range common among them (35.3%), these played smaller roles here. Overall, the model saw your dip in later-year performance as the strongest red flag, outweighing the positives from your earlier grades and GPA. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your later undergraduate or graduate coursework to improve decile rankings, and gathering letters from mentors who can highlight your resilience and growth. Many applicants in similar situations reapply successfully after targeted improvements, so use this as motivation to refine your profile. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
